user_query:
Please read the following files under input/ and design a vendor‑neutral observability and alerting setup for our stack:
- input/services.json
- input/incidents.csv
- input/environments.yaml
- input/slo.yaml
- input/logging_requirements.md

Deliver the following artifacts using only relative paths under output/:
1) output/observability/metrics_plan.md — A concise plan per service that applies the application “Rate, Errors, Duration” method and the infrastructure “Utilization, Saturation, Errors” method. Include key metrics, labels/dimensions to segment by, sampling windows, and example vendor‑agnostic query names. Tailor suggested thresholds and focus areas to our past incidents and the SLOs.

2) output/observability/alerts.yaml — A vendor‑neutral alert policy containing at least 6 alerts that cover:
   - Endpoint availability on each service’s health URL
   - SLO burn‑rate alerts for each service (include fast and slow burn levels)
   - Error‑rate and latency threshold alerts
   Each alert must include: name, service, type (availability|threshold|anomaly|SLO), severity (P1|P2|P3|P4), condition (window/metric/operator/threshold OR burn_rate), for (stabilization window), notify (channels per environment), and runbook (relative path under output/runbooks/...). Ensure severities span P1–P4 and use stabilization windows to reduce flapping. Do not reference any specific vendor or tool names.

3) output/runbooks/payment_errors_high.md — A runnable, vendor‑neutral runbook with the sections: “Alert: payment_errors_high”, “What does this mean?”, “Impact”, “Quick diagnosis” (3–5 concrete checks), “Actions”, and “Escalation”. Link steps to the kinds of evidence an on‑call engineer should check (e.g., recent deploys, dependency status pages, logs, latency/error metrics) without naming specific tools.

4) output/observability/logs_schema.json — A structured logging schema definition including required fields: timestamp, level, service, event, requestId, optional correlationId and userId, and nested http fields (method, path, status), plus duration_ms. Include an “example” object that illustrates a payment failure log with requestId and a non‑200 status.

5) output/observability/dashboards.json — Two dashboard definitions: “applications” and “infrastructure”, each with a panels array of objects (title, query placeholder, unit). Applications must include request rate, error rate, and p95/p99 latency panels. Infrastructure must include CPU, memory, and disk utilization panels.

6) output/README.md — A brief overview explaining alert severities, notification routing per environment, and how to use runbooks to keep alerts actionable and avoid alert fatigue. Keep the entire document vendor‑neutral and reference only the files above.

Use the input data to tailor service names, health URLs, thresholds, and notification routing. Write all files to the specified output/ paths. Avoid any specific vendor or tool names across all deliverables.
